title: The Return of Positional Warfare in the 21st Century

source.original: General Valerii Zaluzhnyi on the Impossibility of Operational-Level Tasks Due to Technological Progress — Thread by Giorgi Revishvili

body_html:
One of the most unsettling conclusions from the war in Ukraine is how closely it resembles the trench warfare of World War I rather than the mobile campaigns that defined military thinking for the past eighty years. The trenches, minefields, and attritional battles along the Donbas front would be grimly familiar to soldiers of 1917 — even if the technology overhead is radically different.

This reversion to positional warfare is not a failure of imagination. It is the logical outcome of a battlefield where defensive technologies — ubiquitous surveillance, precision artillery, loitering munitions, and dense minefields — have outpaced offensive capabilities. The offense-defense balance, which post-Cold War Western doctrine assumed favored the attacker with sufficient combined-arms proficiency, has tilted sharply toward the defense.

Ukraine's 2023 counteroffensive illustrated the problem in brutal clarity. Despite extensive Western training, substantial equipment deliveries, and careful planning, Ukrainian forces struggled to penetrate Russian defensive lines. The fundamental issue was not a lack of courage or competence — it was that every phase of the offensive was observed, contested, and attenuated by an enemy that could see and strike at every step.

The historical parallel to WWI is instructive but not exact. Unlike 1914–1918, today's positional warfare is shaped by precision rather than volume. A single drone operator can halt an armored column; a GPS-guided shell can destroy a command post identified by electronic emissions. The stalemate is technologically mediated in ways that make it potentially even harder to break than its predecessor.

For military planners, the return of positional warfare demands a fundamental reexamination of how offensives are conceived, resourced, and executed. The assumption that training and technology would prevent a return to attritional deadlock has been tested — and found wanting.
